content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
1 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Rebecca L. Moon 
Senior Assistant City Attorney 
456 W. Olive Avenue 
P.O. Box 3707 
Sunnyvale, CA 94088 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-042 
Dear Ms. Moon: 
March 23, 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Sunnyvale Vice Mayor 
Gustav Larsson, Councilmembers Larry Klein and Russ Melton, Planning Commissioners Ken 
Rheaume and Sue Harrison, and Sunnyvale Economic Development Manager Connie Verceles 
regarding their duties under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the 
"Act").1 This request is a follow up to the Boco Advice Letter, No. A-15-078. 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
QUESTION 
May the identified officials make, participate in making, and influence governmental 
decisions related to the adoption the El Camino Real Specific Plan? 
CONCLUSION 
Based on the facts provided, we conclude that the officials will not have conflicts of interest 
in the decisions. 
FACTS 
The El Camino Real Precise Plan (adopted in 1973 and updated in 1993 and 2007) covers 
324 acres along the 4-mile frontage along El Camino Real. El Camino Real is a six-lane state 
highway that bisects the entire City of Sunnyvale in a roughly east-west direction. It is a major 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-17-042 
Page No. 2 
commercial corridor designed primarily for automobile travel, dominated by shopping centers, auto 
dealerships, auto services, hotels, and restaurants, with some high-density housing. 
The 2007 Precise Plan: The purpose of the 2007 Precise Plan was to establish a common 
vision and design guidelines, develop a unified design and distinct aesthetic standards for the 
corridor, and encourage mixed-use (residential and commercial) development in the major "nodes." 
The Precise Plan identified four nodes defined by proximity to the major intersections on El 
Camino Real: Western (Bernardo Avenue), Downtown (Sunnyvale Avenue), Community Center 
(Fair Oaks Avenue) and Eastern (Wolfe Road). The Precise Plan generally identified these locations 
throughout the corridor, but did not specify actual property boundaries where mixed-use 
development should be allowed.2 
Although approximately 80 percent of the 2007 Precise Plan area is commercially zoned 
("C2-ECR"), the City's Zoning Code allows residential or mixed-use projects with a use permit. In 
recent years, the City has approved several mixed-use projects in the Precise Plan area. 
The City's 2015 Housing Element identifies the corridor as one of the key areas for meeting 
the City's share of regional housing needs, with the potential for adding up to 1,201 net new 
residential units. One issue with the Precise Plan, however, is that it encourages mixed-use 
development but does not impose restrictions on the maximum allowabie density of residential uses 
in C2-ECR zones, creating uncertainty about the types of projects that may be approved. 
The Proposed Specific Plan: In July 2015, the City kicked off a project to update the 2007 
Precise Plan and replace it with the Specific Plan. The stated purpose of the Specific Plan is: 
"[T]o refine and guide the future vision of Sunnyvale's EI Camino Real as 
a vibrant, mixed use corridor with improved streetscapes and safer environments 
for walking, bicycling and other modes of transportation. The Plan will update 
and build on the successes of the 2007 Precise Plan for El Camino Real and the 
corridor's assets, and will include a comprehensive strategy to address land use, 
economic vitality, building forms, multimodal connectivity and the streetscape 
(sidewalks, landscaping and public space)." 
The City website further provides: 
"The purpose of a Specific Plan is to provide the City, property owners 
and businesses with a guide for future development. The ECR Plan or Specific 
Plan will not only provide general vision and broad policy concepts to guide 
development, but also provide the details on the type, location and scale of uses 
and define the capacity and design of needed public improvements and 
infrastructure. The Specific Plan will also determine the resources necessary to 
finance and implement the public improvements and infrastructure needed to 
support the vision for the area. The intent is to provide design standards for the 
public realm to give the area a unique identity and the foundation to attract quality 
projects that will enhance the community. The Specific Plan will address market 
2 The areas between the nodes are called "segments." 


File No. A-17-042 
Page No. 3 
constraints and opportunities for a variety of uses, and evaluate the 
appropriateness of other uses necessary to create a vibrant, mixed-use corridor. 
The goal is a Plan that contains policies, land use regulations, design standards, a 
public improvement program and expectations for community benefits." 
The Study Area: The Study Area for the Specific Plan includes all properties within 1/4 mile 
(1,320 feet) of the centerline of El Camino Real. You believe these are the properties that would be 
immediately impacted by development in the Specific Plan area. On March 9, 2016, you clarified 
that the Study Area for the Specific Plan is the same as the "Priority Development Area" defined by 
the Metropolitan Transportation Commission ("MTC") and the Association of Bay Area 
Governments (ABAG).3 MTC defines a "Priority Development Area" ("PDA'') as an area the city 
council or board of supervisors identify as an area for investment, new homes and job growth. To 
become a PDA, an area must be: I) within an existing community; 2) within walking distance of 
frequent transit service; and 3) designated for more housing in a locally adopted plan or identified 
by a local government for future planning and potential growth. 
The Study Area is being funded by a grant from the MTC, and the grant requires the City to 
look at the effects on the properties within the PDA, not just the smaller Specific Plan area which 
will be directly subject to the Specific Plan's land use regulations. The Study Area (2.3 square 
mileĖ) is significantly larger than the actual Specific Plan area (324 acres) which is the same in both 
the Specific Plan and the 2007 Precise Plan. 
Histo,y of the Specific Plan: As part of the development of the Specific Plan, the City 
Council established an Advisory Committee which met regularly throughout 2016. The City also 
held several community workshops. This process resulted in three proposed land-use alternatives, 
all of which retain the concepts of nodes and segments from the 2007 Precise Plan, but more 
specifically define the node boundaries and establish the allowed residential densities for mixed-use 
projects in identified areas. Adoption of the Specific Plan will include rezoning certain parcels 
along El Camino Real from commercial to mixed use. The City may also adopt new zoning 
regulations to prohibit residential uses in some locations or explore other strategies to preserve 
commercial uses within the Specific Plan area. The three alternatives are: 
• Alternative C (Commercial Focus)- emphasizes preservation of the commercial nature 
of El Camino Real. Mixed-use residential designations would be concentrated in the four 
nodes. 
• Alternative M (Mixed-Use Focus) -balances commercial and residential development 
along El Camino Real. Mixed-use residential designations are primarily concentrated in 
the four nodes, but there are additional mixed-use areas within the segments. 
• Alternative R (Residential Focus)- provides more opportunities for residential 
development than the other two alternatives, and residential densities are increased for 
certain sites within the nodes. 
3 MTC is the transportation planning, financing and coordinating agency for the nine-county SF Bay Area; 
ABAG also works to coordinate long tenn planning among Bay Area communities. 


File No. A-17-042 
Page No. 4 
The three alternatives are tentatively scheduled to be considered by the Planning 
Commission on April 24, 2017 and City Council on May 16, 2017 for preliminary direction (not 
adoption). At that time, staff will ask the Planning Commission and City Council each to select an 
alternative for further study. Staff will return to the City Council later with recommendations for 
adoption of the Specific Plan following finalization of the Specific Plan document and preparation 
of an environmental impact report which will fully evaluate the environmental impacts of the 
proposed Specific Plan. 
You note that the scope of development encouraged by the proposed alternatives for the 
Specific Plan (particularly the "mixed use" and "residential focus" alternatives) may, over time, 
result in increased volume and intensity of traffic on El Camino Real. However, one of the primary 
goals of the Specific Plan is to reduce traffic by transforming El Camino Real into a modern 
thoroughfare that is friendly to walking, biking and mass transit. To that end, the Specific Plan will 
include policies and standards to mitigate traffic impacts through streetscape improvements, such as 
bike lanes, wider sidewalks, better landscaping, safer intersections, and improved transit stops, as 
well as transportation demand management strategies to reduce peak-hour vehicle trips. 
The Public Officials' Interests 
1. Economic Development Manager Connie V erceles: Ms. Verceles owns a home on 
Andover Drive, located south of El Camino Real within the Study Area and 364 feet outside the 
Specific Plan area boundary. Ms. Verceles' property is approximately one block southeast of the 
Western Node, which is a retail district centered around the intersection of El Camino Real and 
Bernardo A venue. The area is anchored by a large Safeway shopping center. 
The stretch of El Camino Real to the east of the Western Node ("Segment A") is dominated 
by auto dealerships and auto-service businesses. The entire area is currently zoned commercial (C2-
ECR). A large mixed-use project (medical office and apartments) was recently completed on the 
north side of El Camino Real. As noted above, mixed-use projects can be approved in C2-ECR with 
a use permit, and are encouraged by the 2007 Precise Plan. 
Under all three alternatives, the Western Node would be rezoned from commercial to 
medium- and high-density mixed use. Segment A will remain primarily commercial. The "mixed 
use" and "residential focus" alternatives identify additional sites in Segment A that would be 
appropriate for medium density mixed use development (24 dwelling units/acre). 
2. Vice Mayor Larsson, Councilmember Klein, and Commissioner Rheaume: Each of 
these officials own single-family homes north of El Camino Real near the Downtown Node. 
• Vice Mayor Larsson and Commissioner Rheaume both live on Bishop Avenue in a single­
family neighborhood approximately two blocks north of El Camino Real. The closest point 
of Vice Mayor Larsson's property to the Downtown Node boundary is 786 feet, and the 
closest point of Commissioner Rheaume's property is 775 feet away. Both homes are 
outside the Study Area, and separated from the businesses on El Camino Real by a block of 
single-family homes and a medical-office complex on Old San Francisco Road (not part of 
the plan area). 


File No. A-17-042 
Page No. 5 
• Councilmember Klein's property is on South Frances Street, within the Study Area, and 55 
feet from the boundary of the Downtown Node. Klein's home is separated by one 
neighboring house from a narrow commercial strip that includes the Rooster T. Feathers 
Comedy Club. The City installed traffic barriers on South Frances approximately 20 years 
ago because of neighborhood complaints about traffic and parking. The barricades block 
cut-through traffic from El Camino Real and discourage patrons of the comedy Comedy 
Club and other nearby businesses from parking in the residential neighborhood. 
The Downtown Node is centered on the intersection of El Camino Real and Sunnyvale 
Avenue. The area is currently zoned commercial {C2-ECR) except for the Sunnyvale Civic Center, 
which is public facilities (PF). As discussed, residential uses are allowed in the C2-ECR 
commercial zone with a use permit, and some mixed-use redevelopment has already occurred in the 
Downtown Node. In the early 2000's, a large mixed-use project (retail and apartments) was built on 
the southeast corner of El Camino Real and Mathilda, known as the Cherry Orchard. There is also a 
townhome development across the street on the southwest side of El Camino Real and Mathilda 
adjoining the Las Palmas Park and Tennis Center. A mixed-use project that includes a Courtyard 
Marriot Hotel and 103 townhomes was recently completed in the same area. 
Under all three alternatives, the.commercial strip containing the Rooster T. Feathers 
Comedy Club would retain its commercial designation between Taaffe Street and Sunnyvale 
Avenue. The rest of the Downtown Node (other than the Civic Center) would be rezoned to mixed 
use. The only differences between the alternatives is that the "residential focus" alternative would 
allow for slightly higher-density mixed use in the southwest comer of El Camino Real and Mathilda 
(36 vs. 24 dwelling units/acre), which is currently a small retail area adjoining townhouse 
developments. 
3. Councilmcmber Russ Melton and Planning Commissioner Sue Harrison: Both 
officials own homes south of El Camino Real in the vicinity of the Eastern Node. 
• Commissioner Harrison's home is on Norman Drive, within the Study Area 
and 271 feet from the Specific Plan boundary. 
• Councilmember Melton's home is on Hampton Drive outside the Study Area, 
1,730 feet from the Specific Plan boundary. 
The Eastern Node is centered around the intersection of El Camino Real and Wolfe Road. 
This area includes a mixture of uses including hotels, townhomes, apartments, auto services, an RV 
Park, a municipal golf course, miniature golf, senior apartments, and a closed mobile home park. A 
large mixed-use project was recently approved on the southwest comer of El Camino Real and 
Wolfe ("Butcher's Comer") and a hotel is under construction on the northwest corner of the 
intersection. 
The existing zoning in the Eastern Node includes commercial (C2-ECR), medium-density 
residential (R3-ECR) and high-density residential (R4-ECR). The half-mile stretch east of the 
Eastern Node ("Segment C") includes a mixture of retail, restaurants, rental apartments, and 
townhouse developments. 


File No. A-17-042 
Page No. 6 
None of the three alternatives would change the existing zoning of the Eastern Node south 
of El Camino Real and east of Wolfe Road, which is the section nearest to Councilmember 
Melton's and Commissioner Harrison's properties. The northeast corner of the node (currently an 
apartment complex, townhomes, and a car wash) would be rezoned for high-density mixed use. 
The existing zoning in Segment C would not change under the "commercial focus" alternative. The 
"mixed use" alternative would rezone the closed mobile home park on the corner of Poplar and El 
Camino Real from commercial to high-density mixed use (36 dwelling units/acre). The "residential 
focus" alternative identifies some additional sites in Segment C that would be appropriate for 
mixed-use development. 
You also noted that all the Public Officials' properties, except for Councilmember Klein's 
property, are buffered from the Specific Plan area by at least one or more blocks of existing single­
family homes. On February 28, 2017, you provided additional facts: 
• The current plan is an update to the 2007 Precise Plan, but will be called a specific plan 
which means that it will be governed by the requirements for specific plans in Sections 
65450-65457. "Precise Plan" is a vaguer term not defined by state law. In Sunnyvale, a 
precise plan is somewhat less comprehensive than a specific plan. For example, the 2007 
Precise Plan established general goals and policies to encourage more mixed-use 
development in the plan area. The proposed Specific Plan would go a step further by 
specifically identifying parcels suitable for mixed-use development as well as potentially 
rezoning properties from commercial to mixed use.4 
• The Specific Plan area is the same as the 2007 Precise Plan area. The "Study Area" is a 
larger area that was selected as the area of focus for the Specific Plan study. However, only 
the Specific Plan area will be subject to the development standards contained in the adopted 
Specific Plan. 
• You also noted that the Specific Plan area established by the 2007 Precise Plan is not 
changing, nor are the goals of the Precise Plan ( e.g. to encourage more mixed use 
development, especially in the "nodes"). The type of development allowed in the Specific 
Plan area is not changing (as noted in your letter, mixed-use development can currently be 
built in the Specific Plan area with a use permit). However, under the proposed Specific 
Plan, the areas where the City wants to encourage mixed use will be more clearly defined 
and potentially rezoned to mixed use. 
ANALYSIS 
Each of the officials has a financial interest in real property worth at last $2,000. (Section 
87103(b).) 
4 As you note in your letter requesting advice, we previously advised that many of the officials in question did 
not have conflicts of interest with respect to the "general goals and policies" of the 2007 Precise Plan. However, we 
cautioned that "if the consideration of the Update progresses to a point where more concrete applications or projects are 
considered near the officials' respective properties, you should contact us for further advice." (Boco Advice Letter, No. 
A-15-078.) In connection with this request, you stated that the proposed Specific Plan will go a step further [than the 
2007 Precise Plan] by specifically identifying parcels suitable for mixed-use development as well as potentially 
rezoning properties from commercial to mixed use, warranting a more thorough and specific analysis of the issues. 


Foreseeability and Materiality 
File No. A-17-042 
Page No. 7 
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a 
specific governmental decision (here, whether to adopt the Specific Plan) on a public official's 
interests is material. The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is 
explicitly involved in the decision. Regulation 18701 (a) provides: 
"A financial effect on a financial interest is presumed to be reasonably 
foreseeable if the financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a 
governmental decision before the official or the official's agency. A financial 
interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, 
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement 
to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision 
affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation 
l 8702.2(a)(l )-(6)."5 
None of the standards set forth above apply to your facts. None of the officials own property within 
the actual Specific Plan area which is the subject of the decision. 
If property interests are "not explicitly involved," as here, a financial effect is only 
reasonably foreseeable if the effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than 
hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701(b).) 
Regulation 18702.2 provides the materiality standards applicable to an official's real­
property interest. Having already disposed of Regulation 18 702 .2( a )(1 )-( 6), the remaining pertinent 
standards are: 
"(10) Would change the character of the parcel ofreal property by 
substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of 
property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise 
levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the 
market value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial 
interest; 
*** 
"(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and 
consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision 
5 Regulation 18702.2 applies to decisions such as: ( 1) the adoption of or amendment to a general or specific 
plan that includes the official's property; (2) decisions to zone or rezone, annex or deannex, or include or exclude the 
official's property from any city, county, district, or other local government subdivision; (3) imposing or changing any 
taxes, fees, or assessments on the official's property; (4) authorizing the sale, purchase, or lease oflhe official's 
property; (5) issuance, denial or revocation of a license, pennit or other land-use entitlement applicable to the official's 
property; or (6) construction of, or improvements to, streets, water, sewer, stonn drainage or similar facilities 
benefitting the official's property. 


File No. A-1 7-042 
Page No. 8 
was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the 
market value of the official's property." 
The officials have the following property interests: 
Official (from the most distant to the closest) 
Councilmember Melton 
Distance 
Vice Mayor Larsson 
Planning Commissioner Rheaume 
Economic Development Manager Verceles 
Planning Commissioner Harrison 
Councilmember Klein 
Councilmcmbcr Melton/Eastern Node 
1,730 
786 
775 
364 
271 
55 
Closest Node 
Eastern 
Downtown 
Downtown 
Western 
Eastern 
Downtown 
• 
Councilmember Melton's property is 1,730 feet from the closest boundary of the Eastern 
Node and is outside the Study Area. It is separated from the Specific Plan boundary by 
several residential housing blocks and is buffered from the changes in the Eastern Node by 
multiple blocks of houses and businesses between the home and El Camino Real. In 
addition, none of the three alternatives would change the existing zoning of property south 
of El Camino Real and east of Wolfe Road, which is the section of the Specific Pian nearest 
to Councilmember Melton's property. 
• 
We also note that the Specific Plan, while making specific changes in the allowable use of 
property, would not change the actual current use of property in the plan area. You stated 
that the adoption of the Specific Plan will not authorize construction of any specific projects 
and actual redevelopment under the Specific Plan will occur gradually over many years as 
existing buildings are sold or reach the end of their useful life span. Moreover, specific 
future governmental decisions will be necessary for the approval of any future projects and 
will involve a separate evaluation of any potential conflicts of interest for affected City 
officials. Until such projects are proposed it is not foreseeable that tangible effects (such as 
changes in traffic or the intensity of use in the area) can be definitively evaluated.6 (See 
e.g., Chopra Advice Letter, No. A-16-139.) 
Based on the totality of the facts, including the distances between the Specific Plan area and 
Councilmember Melton's property, we conclude that it is not foreseeable that the property will be 
materially financially affected by the decisions. 
6 However, while we are mindful that the actual development of the property will not be a part of the current 
decision, it is certain that if the rezoning does not occur, such a change in use cannot occur. Therefore, this decision 
could be considered a necessary prerequisite to ultimate development of the property and the future use cannot be 
disregarded. 


Vice Mayor Larsson and Planning Commissioner Rheaume 
File No. A-I 7-042 
Page No. 9 
• Vice Mayor Larsson's property is 786 feet from the Downtown Node. Planning 
Commissioner Rheaume's property is 775 feet from the Downtown Node. While each is 
within the Study Area, they are at the outer edge. Both properties are separated from the 
businesses on El Camino Real by a block of single-family homes and a medical office 
complex on Old San Francisco Road (that is not part of the Specific Plan area). 
Again, based on the distance and substantial buffer between these officials' respective 
properties and the Specific Plan area, we conclude that neither Vice Mayor Larsson nor 
Commissioner Rheaume's respective properties will be materially affected by the decisions. 
The final three Public Officials all own their respective properties within 500 feet of the 
Specific Plan area and would also be subject to Regulation l 8702.2(a)(l 1) in addition to Regulation 
18702.2(a)(IO) and (12). Regulation 18702.2(a)(l l )  provides: 
"Would consider any decision affecting real property value located within 
500 feet of the property line of the official's real property, other than commercial 
property containing a business entity where the materiality standards are analyzed 
under Regulation 18702.1. Notwithstanding this prohibition, the Commission may 
provide written advice allowing an official to participate under these 
circumstances if the Commission determines that there are sufficient facts to 
indicate that there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the 
official's property ... " 
Economic Development Manager Vcrceles/ Western Node 
• Economic Development Manager Verceles' property is 364 feet away from the Western 
Node. 
• Ms. Verceles' property is approximately one block southeast of the Western Node, a retail 
district centered around the intersection of El Camino Real and Bernardo A venue. The area 
is anchored by a large Safeway shopping center and is currently zoned commercial (C2-
ECR). 
• Under all three alternatives, the Western Node would be rezoned from commercial to 
medium- and high-density mixed use. Segment A will remain primarily commercial. The 
"mixed use" and "residential focus" alternatives identify additional sites in Segment A that 
would be appropriate for medium density mixed use development (24 dwelling units/acre). 
The treatment of the Western Node is similar under each of the three alternatives. Under any 
of the alternatives, the current zoning is changed from commercial to medium- and high-density 
mixed use. However, we note that a large mixed-use project has already been approved for property 
just north of El Camino Real. In addition, a large part of the node near Ms. Verceles' property has 
an established Safeway shopping center that would likely not be changed to a mixed-use project in 
the near future. While the zoning change will apply to the rest of the node, you noted that in those 
areas, no changes in use will occur unless a proposed project is submitted for additional City 


File No. A-1 7-042 
Page No. 10 
review. Thus, we conclude that the instant decisions will not have a foreseeable and material 
financial effect the value of Ms. Verceles' property. 
Planning Commissioner Harrison/Eastern Node 
• Planning Commissioner Harrison owns property within 271 feet of the Eastern Node. 
However, none of the three alternatives would change the existing zoning of property south 
of El Camino Real and east of Wolfe Road, which is the section nearest to the Planning 
Commissioner's property. However, the northeast comer of the node (currently an 
apartment complex, townhomes, and a car wash) would be rezoned for high-density mixed 
use, which is approximately 600 feet from the Commissioner's home. The change in zoning 
is significant, yet impacts on the Commissioner's property are buffered by at least two 
developed City blocks and El Camino Real itself. 
Thus, we conclude that the rezoning decisions will not have a material financial effect on 
the Planning Commissioner Harrison's property. 
Councilmember Klein/Downtown Node 
• 
Councilmember Klein owns property within 55 feet of the Downtown Node. 
• 
Under all three alternatives, the commercial strip (the area of the node nearest to 
Councilmember Klein's property) would retain its commercial designation between Taaffe 
Street and Sunnyvale Avenue. The rest of the downtown node (other than the Civic Center) 
would be rezoned to mixed use. The only differences between the alternatives is that the 
"residential focus" alternative would allow for slightly higher-density mixed use in the 
southwest comer of El Camino Real and Mathilda (36 vs. 24 dwelling units/acre), which is 
currently a small retail area adjoining townhouse developments. 
Councilmember Klein's residence is only 55 feet from the boundary of the Downtown 
Node. The Downtown Node is the largest node in the Specific Plan area and virtually all of it will 
be rezoned from commercial to mixed use. However, as you noted, the zoning changes will not 
result in any immediate changes in the use of the properties near his home. If projects are proposed, 
they will go through additional City review and consideration. 
Additionally, you noted that none of the three alternatives propose changing the zoning 
applicable to the existing commercial strip closest to Councilmember Klein's property. You also 
noted that approximately 20 years ago, the City installed traffic barriers on South Frances because 
of neighborhood complaints about traffic and parking. The barricades block cut-through traffic from 
El Camino Real and discourage patrons of the Comedy Club and other nearby businesses from 
parking in the residential neighborhood. You stated that this will shield Councilmember Klein's 
home from increased traffic that may be caused by residents commuting to the high-tech businesses 
in north Sunnyvale. And the effect of the zoning changes will also be buffered by the fact that they 
will all occur on the other side of El Camino Real. 
Based on these buffers and the the fact that no changes in use will occur without a proposed 
project going through additional City review, we conclude that the decisions relating to the Specific 


File No. A-17-042 
Page No. 11 
Plan will not have a foreseeable and material financial effect the value of Councilmember Klein's 
property. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
JWW:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General-Counsel 
Assistan General Counsel 
Legal D vision

sections.question:
May the identified officials make, participate in making, and influence governmental 
decisions related to the adoption the El Camino Real Specific Plan?
sections.conclusion:
Based on the facts provided, we conclude that the officials will not have conflicts of interest 
in the decisions.
sections.facts:

constraints and opportunities for a variety of uses, and evaluate the

The goal is a Plan that contains policies, land use regulations, design standards, a

(1,320 feet) of the centerline of El Camino Real. You believe these are the properties that would be 
immediately impacted by development in the Specific Plan area. On March 9, 2016, you clarified 
that the Study Area for the Specific Plan is the same as the "Priority Development Area" defined by 
the Metropolitan Transportation Commission ("MTC") and the Association of Bay Area 
Governments (ABAG).3 MTC defines a "Priority Development Area" ("PDA'') as an area the city 
council or board of supervisors identify as an area for investment, new homes and job growth. To 
become a PDA, an area must be: I) within an existing community; 2) within walking distance of 
frequent transit service; and 3) designated for more housing in a locally adopted plan or identified 
by a local government for future planning and potential growth. 
The Study Area is being funded by a grant from the MTC, and the grant requires the City to 
look at the effects on the properties within the PDA, not just the smaller Specific Plan area which 
will be directly subject to the Specific Plan's land use regulations. The Study Area (2.3 square 
mileĖ) is significantly larger than the actual Specific Plan area (324 acres) which is the same in both 
the Specific Plan and the 2007 Precise Plan. 
Histo,y of the Specific Plan: As part of the development of the Specific Plan, the City 
Council established an Advisory Committee which met regularly throughout 2016. The City also 
held several community workshops. This process resulted in three proposed land-use alternatives, 
all of which retain the concepts of nodes and segments from the 2007 Precise Plan, but more 
specifically define the node boundaries and establish the allowed residential densities for mixed-use 
projects in identified areas. Adoption of the Specific Plan will include rezoning certain parcels 
along El Camino Real from commercial to mixed use. The City may also adopt new zoning 
regulations to prohibit residential uses in some locations or explore other strategies to preserve 
commercial uses within the Specific Plan area. The three alternatives are: 
• Alternative C (Commercial Focus)- emphasizes preservation of the commercial nature 
of El Camino Real. Mixed-use residential designations would be concentrated in the four 
nodes. 
• Alternative M (Mixed-Use Focus) -balances commercial and residential development 
along El Camino Real. Mixed-use residential designations are primarily concentrated in 
the four nodes, but there are additional mixed-use areas within the segments. 
• Alternative R (Residential Focus)- provides more opportunities for residential 
development than the other two alternatives, and residential densities are increased for 
certain sites within the nodes. 
3 MTC is the transportation planning, financing and coordinating agency for the nine-county SF Bay Area; 
ABAG also works to coordinate long tenn planning among Bay Area communities.  
The three alternatives are tentatively scheduled to be considered by the Planning 
Commission on April 24, 2017 and City Council on May 16, 2017 for preliminary direction (not 
adoption). At that time, staff will ask the Planning Commission and City Council each to select an 
alternative for further study. Staff will return to the City Council later with recommendations for 
adoption of the Specific Plan following finalization of the Specific Plan document and preparation 
of an environmental impact report which will fully evaluate the environmental impacts of the 
proposed Specific Plan. 
You note that the scope of development encouraged by the proposed alternatives for the 
Specific Plan (particularly the "mixed use" and "residential focus" alternatives) may, over time, 
result in increased volume and intensity of traffic on El Camino Real. However, one of the primary 
goals of the Specific Plan is to reduce traffic by transforming El Camino Real into a modern 
thoroughfare that is friendly to walking, biking and mass transit. To that end, the Specific Plan will 
include policies and standards to mitigate traffic impacts through streetscape improvements, such as 
bike lanes, wider sidewalks, better landscaping, safer intersections, and improved transit stops, as 
well as transportation demand management strategies to reduce peak-hour vehicle trips. 
The Public Officials' Interests 
1. Economic Development Manager Connie V erceles: Ms. Verceles owns a home on 
Andover Drive, located south of El Camino Real within the Study Area and 364 feet outside the 
Specific Plan area boundary. Ms. Verceles' property is approximately one block southeast of the 
Western Node, which is a retail district centered around the intersection of El Camino Real and 
Bernardo A venue. The area is anchored by a large Safeway shopping center. 
The stretch of El Camino Real to the east of the Western Node ("Segment A") is dominated 
by auto dealerships and auto-service businesses. The entire area is currently zoned commercial (C2-
ECR). A large mixed-use project (medical office and apartments) was recently completed on the 
north side of El Camino Real. As noted above, mixed-use projects can be approved in C2-ECR with 
a use permit, and are encouraged by the 2007 Precise Plan. 
Under all three alternatives, the Western Node would be rezoned from commercial to 
medium- and high-density mixed use. Segment A will remain primarily commercial. The "mixed 
use" and "residential focus" alternatives identify additional sites in Segment A that would be 
appropriate for medium density mixed use development (24 dwelling units/acre). 
2. Vice Mayor Larsson, Councilmember Klein, and Commissioner Rheaume: Each of 
these officials own single-family homes north of El Camino Real near the Downtown Node. 
• Vice Mayor Larsson and Commissioner Rheaume both live on Bishop Avenue in a single­
family neighborhood approximately two blocks north of El Camino Real. The closest point 
of Vice Mayor Larsson's property to the Downtown Node boundary is 786 feet, and the 
closest point of Commissioner Rheaume's property is 775 feet away. Both homes are 
outside the Study Area, and separated from the businesses on El Camino Real by a block of 
single-family homes and a medical-office complex on Old San Francisco Road (not part of 
the plan area).  
• Councilmember Klein's property is on South Frances Street, within the Study Area, and 55 
feet from the boundary of the Downtown Node. Klein's home is separated by one 
neighboring house from a narrow commercial strip that includes the Rooster T. Feathers 
Comedy Club. The City installed traffic barriers on South Frances approximately 20 years 
ago because of neighborhood complaints about traffic and parking. The barricades block 
cut-through traffic from El Camino Real and discourage patrons of the comedy Comedy 
Club and other nearby businesses from parking in the residential neighborhood. 
The Downtown Node is centered on the intersection of El Camino Real and Sunnyvale 
Avenue. The area is currently zoned commercial {C2-ECR) except for the Sunnyvale Civic Center, 
which is public facilities (PF). As discussed, residential uses are allowed in the C2-ECR 
commercial zone with a use permit, and some mixed-use redevelopment has already occurred in the 
Downtown Node. In the early 2000's, a large mixed-use project (retail and apartments) was built on 
the southeast corner of El Camino Real and Mathilda, known as the Cherry Orchard. There is also a 
townhome development across the street on the southwest side of El Camino Real and Mathilda 
adjoining the Las Palmas Park and Tennis Center. A mixed-use project that includes a Courtyard 
Marriot Hotel and 103 townhomes was recently completed in the same area. 
Under all three alternatives, the.commercial strip containing the Rooster T. Feathers 
Comedy Club would retain its commercial designation between Taaffe Street and Sunnyvale 
Avenue. The rest of the Downtown Node (other than the Civic Center) would be rezoned to mixed 
use. The only differences between the alternatives is that the "residential focus" alternative would 
allow for slightly higher-density mixed use in the southwest comer of El Camino Real and Mathilda 
(36 vs. 24 dwelling units/acre), which is currently a small retail area adjoining townhouse 
developments. 
3. Councilmcmber Russ Melton and Planning Commissioner Sue Harrison: Both 
officials own homes south of El Camino Real in the vicinity of the Eastern Node. 
• Commissioner Harrison's home is on Norman Drive, within the Study Area 
and 271 feet from the Specific Plan boundary. 
• Councilmember Melton's home is on Hampton Drive outside the Study Area, 
1,730 feet from the Specific Plan boundary. 
The Eastern Node is centered around the intersection of El Camino Real and Wolfe Road. 
This area includes a mixture of uses including hotels, townhomes, apartments, auto services, an RV 
Park, a municipal golf course, miniature golf, senior apartments, and a closed mobile home park. A 
large mixed-use project was recently approved on the southwest comer of El Camino Real and 
Wolfe ("Butcher's Comer") and a hotel is under construction on the northwest corner of the 
intersection. 
The existing zoning in the Eastern Node includes commercial (C2-ECR), medium-density 
residential (R3-ECR) and high-density residential (R4-ECR). The half-mile stretch east of the 
Eastern Node ("Segment C") includes a mixture of retail, restaurants, rental apartments, and 
townhouse developments.  
None of the three alternatives would change the existing zoning of the Eastern Node south 
of El Camino Real and east of Wolfe Road, which is the section nearest to Councilmember 
Melton's and Commissioner Harrison's properties. The northeast corner of the node (currently an 
apartment complex, townhomes, and a car wash) would be rezoned for high-density mixed use. 
The existing zoning in Segment C would not change under the "commercial focus" alternative. The 
"mixed use" alternative would rezone the closed mobile home park on the corner of Poplar and El 
Camino Real from commercial to high-density mixed use (36 dwelling units/acre). The "residential 
focus" alternative identifies some additional sites in Segment C that would be appropriate for 
mixed-use development. 
You also noted that all the Public Officials' properties, except for Councilmember Klein's 
property, are buffered from the Specific Plan area by at least one or more blocks of existing single­
family homes. On February 28, 2017, you provided additional facts: 
• The current plan is an update to the 2007 Precise Plan, but will be called a specific plan 
which means that it will be governed by the requirements for specific plans in Sections 
65450-65457. "Precise Plan" is a vaguer term not defined by state law. In Sunnyvale, a 
precise plan is somewhat less comprehensive than a specific plan. For example, the 2007 
Precise Plan established general goals and policies to encourage more mixed-use 
development in the plan area. The proposed Specific Plan would go a step further by 
specifically identifying parcels suitable for mixed-use development as well as potentially 
rezoning properties from commercial to mixed use.4 
• The Specific Plan area is the same as the 2007 Precise Plan area. The "Study Area" is a 
larger area that was selected as the area of focus for the Specific Plan study. However, only 
the Specific Plan area will be subject to the development standards contained in the adopted 
Specific Plan. 
• You also noted that the Specific Plan area established by the 2007 Precise Plan is not 
changing, nor are the goals of the Precise Plan ( e.g. to encourage more mixed use 
development, especially in the "nodes"). The type of development allowed in the Specific 
Plan area is not changing (as noted in your letter, mixed-use development can currently be 
built in the Specific Plan area with a use permit). However, under the proposed Specific 
Plan, the areas where the City wants to encourage mixed use will be more clearly defined 
and potentially rezoned to mixed use.
sections.analysis:
Each of the officials has a financial interest in real property worth at last $2,000. (Section 
87103(b).) 
4 As you note in your letter requesting advice, we previously advised that many of the officials in question did 
not have conflicts of interest with respect to the "general goals and policies" of the 2007 Precise Plan. However, we 
cautioned that "if the consideration of the Update progresses to a point where more concrete applications or projects are 
considered near the officials' respective properties, you should contact us for further advice." (Boco Advice Letter, No. 
A-15-078.) In connection with this request, you stated that the proposed Specific Plan will go a step further [than the 
2007 Precise Plan] by specifically identifying parcels suitable for mixed-use development as well as potentially 
rezoning properties from commercial to mixed use, warranting a more thorough and specific analysis of the issues. 


Foreseeability and Materiality  
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a 
specific governmental decision (here, whether to adopt the Specific Plan) on a public official's 
interests is material. The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is 
explicitly involved in the decision. Regulation 18701 (a) provides: 
"A financial effect on a financial interest is presumed to be reasonably 
foreseeable if the financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a 
governmental decision before the official or the official's agency. A financial 
interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, 
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement 
to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision 
affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation 
l 8702.2(a)(l )-(6)."5 
None of the standards set forth above apply to your facts. None of the officials own property within 
the actual Specific Plan area which is the subject of the decision. 
If property interests are "not explicitly involved," as here, a financial effect is only 
reasonably foreseeable if the effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than 
hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701(b).) 
Regulation 18702.2 provides the materiality standards applicable to an official's real­
property interest. Having already disposed of Regulation 18 702 .2( a )(1 )-( 6), the remaining pertinent 
standards are: 
"(10) Would change the character of the parcel ofreal property by 
substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of 
property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise 
levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the 
market value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial 
interest;

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May the identified officials make, participate in making, and influence governmental 
decisions related to the adoption the El Camino Real Specific Plan?

CONCLUSION: Based on the facts provided, we conclude that the officials will not have conflicts of interest 
in the decisions.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION • 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 1 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 Rebecca L. Moon Senior Assistant City Attorney 456 W. Olive Avenue P.O. Box 3707 Sunnyvale, CA 94088 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-17-042 Dear Ms. Moon: March 23, 2017 This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Sunnyvale Vice Mayor Gustav Larsson, Councilmembers Larry Klein and Russ Melton, Planning Commissioners Ken Rheaume and Sue Harrison, and Sunnyvale Economic Development Manager Connie Verceles regarding their duties under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 This request is a follow up to the Boco Advice Letter, No. A-15-078. Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. QUESTION May the identified officials make, participate in making, and influence governmental decisions related to the adoption the El Camino Real Specific Plan? CONCLUSION Based on the facts provided, we conclude that the officials will not have conflicts of interest in the decisions. FACTS The El Camino Real Precise Plan (adopted in 1973 and updated in 1993 and 2007) covers 324 acres along the 4-mile frontage along El Camino Real. El Camino Real is a six-lane state highway that bisects the entire City of Sunnyvale in a roughly east-west direction. It is a major 1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-17-042 Page No. 2 commercial corridor designed primarily for automobile travel, dominated by shopping centers, auto dealerships, auto services, hotels, and restaurants, with some high-density housing. The 2007 Precise Plan: The purpose of the 2007 Precise Plan was to establish a common vision and design guidelines, develop a unified design and distinct aesthetic standards for the corridor, and encourage mixed-use (residential and commercial) development in the major "nodes." The Precise Plan identified four nodes defined by proximity to the major intersections on El Camino Real: Western (Bernardo Avenue), Downtown (Sunnyvale Avenue), Community Center (Fair Oaks Avenue) and Eastern (Wolfe Road). The Precise Plan generally identified these locations throughout the corridor, but did not specify actual property boundaries where mixed-use development should be allowed.2 Although approximately 80 percent of the 2007 Precise Plan area is commercially zoned ("C2-ECR"), the City's Zoning Code allows residential or mixed-use projects with a use permit. In recent years, the City has approved several mixed-use projects in the Precise Plan area. The City's 2015 Housing Element identifies the corridor as one of the
